Hey, it's Andre and Sean. We are doing a soap opera series review today for the art of email workshop. This one was submitted by Julie. We'll provide a link so you can see a version of this document as well. Julie has written to sat down and button chair and written, she's written 26 pages, which is extraordinary. So first of all, congratulations to Julie for really doing the work. Second of all, though, we're not going to review 26 pages. We want to be clear. It's just that's not going to be helpful to anybody and we would end up jamming a knife in our eyes if we had to do that. So what we're going to do instead is we're going to we're just going to jump around a little bit. We're going to give some more of a high level. I say this like we have any idea. We have no idea what we're going to do. We're just going to something something's about to happen. I guess it's the best way to think about this. So I'm going to get started. I'm going to hand it off to Andre and we're going to ping pong back and forth. And then at some point, one of us is going to say no loss and pass out. And then we're going to click stop record. All right. So a couple of things I notice immediately. And just by way of context, I've looked at this a little bit, but I haven't read all 26 pages and there's a reason for that. Like I always want to have the readers first experience when I look and when I do a review, it's as much as possible. I want I want to not have I don't want to have read something and then thought about it and then come back to it and given feedback on it. That's a very different feedback than if you just read something. And immediately, because most people who get your emails, the initial reaction is their reaction. So that's that's kind of what I'm trying to replicate here. All right. So here are a few things that are sort of high level. One right in the beginning, Julie says my product is a signature 12 a course aimed at women 40 plus to reclaim and increase their physical strength, etc. etc. This is really important because, as we've said in the past, all roads lead to Rome, right? So there is a product somewhere in the future. There's something that this is all about that this the soap opera series doesn't lead directly to the product. That's not what a soap opera series does, but it certainly sets up the context for it sets up. It does two things, one, it pulls people forward who were going to be a good fit for that course at some point in the future. And it discourages people suddenly over time who aren't. And both of those responsibilities are equally valuable. And knowing knowing that sort of future destination makes that filtering in and out much easier. So to see this right up front, this is my product, this is what I'm doing very important. I love how Julie has broken it into three parts Act one, Act two and Act three. Just as a thinking tool, I think that's that's very helpful. I do this a lot as a right notes to myself. Especially if I'm thinking about something over time and I'm not writing it immediately when I'm thinking about it, I'm constructing it, I like to put some notes in. Often I do that with with pencil and paper, not on the screen itself. And you'll also see it truly has things in red here where like she's she's giving all caps value. The magic happens outside go outside where play and move who gets to say how or where we move like this is. I like it's like this notes to herself kind of thing like this is this reminder. This is what this thing is about. Right. And there's it's such a it's so easy to forget what the thing you're writing is about. And we all do it. Especially if we, you know, we're writing about one thing and then something interesting appears in our mind and we start pulling on a thread. And the next thing you know, you know, we look up and we realize we're three miles down a side road and we forgot what the thing we were writing was supposed to be about. It's not off off always that explicit. Sometimes we don't know we've done that until later when we get feedback and realize that we've done that. So I love just my initial overview of this is I love structure. And I love how Julie has integrated some structure with the three acts. I love how she has made some notes to herself to set the stage. She she she starts to tear up inside of you if we if we scroll down a bit. Yeah, and I'll look before we scroll down, I'll comment on a couple of things here too, that just thinking out loud stuff. Read, read, I say this all the time, read your stuff out loud, and then make the the, and I took Andre's point, yes, this is a shitty first draft. So keep that in mind, but read your stuff out loud, and then make the the spacing, and like make make the make everything beautiful to the ear. Because we this is an observation, I think I've heard from Robert Fritz. Yes, it was Robert Fritz. He said, we often we often write for the eye, like how does it look on the page? Well, but people are hearing the voice, most people who are reading, I know this is true for me, are hearing our voice in our head reading it out loud. It's not, you're not hearing the sound, but you're hearing a voice in your in your head reading. So don't just write for the eye, write for the ear. So if I if I, but the thing that jumped out at me as I as I read these these first few sentences is listen to this out loud. I was too lazy and motivated to join a club, do sport again or buy any equipment. And no, you wouldn't find me in a gym. I felt pretty average. That's how it's written. But certainly when you get to, I felt pretty average, there's a there's a pause, right? Like there's, it's I felt pretty dot dot dot average, because that's how it sounds, right? That's you would pause there because you could imagine Julia reflected, right? She's kind of giving you the background. I was too lazy, I was unmotivated. I wasn't going to join a club. I wasn't going to be involved in athletics. I'm going to buy equipment. You weren't there's no way in hell you're going to find me in a gym. Right? That's like the setup. And the resolution is I felt pretty average. Right? That's how it sounds. And that's so you want to write it that way. When you said that I was thinking like, eight bullets is five too many. I think the first thought that popped into my head when I saw that, or actually, but in, I don't, I think these bullets are given, there's too much here. I would, I think I would just get rid of them entirely. Because you're, you're, when we get, I have a little bit of a way to think about this, there should be like a staircase of tension being resolved, or sorry, tension being created in this email. And it's ever rising. You know, and yes, you can have like a little undulation, but in general, the staircase wants to rise step by step by step through the entirety of the series. Now, because we open and close loops along the way, there is some undulation of that tension, which would show up in an oscilloscope actually, Andre, my turn to throw a oscilloscope in, right? But, but really, if we, when we, when we measure it, if we were to look at it on a graph over time, and so let's just say we have an X Y graph, the horizontal line is time, the vertical line is tension. From beginning to end, we want there to be an ever rising tension. And these, this bulleted list just tears the tension out of this, just was, it doesn't set up tension. It tells me things that don't create tension. And there are a lot of them together, which tends to repulse my attention a little bit. Like these are all things that should be told to me over the remainder of the, the series. Don't tell them everything at once because now, like I know too much, there's no mystery. I know too much now. Like there's like, there's, I kind of know what's coming. And not in ways, like when it, here's a simple sentence, 10 minutes of movement is enough to change how you feel and improve cognitive performance. There's no mystery in that. You've told me everything there is about it. I'm like, okay, cool. I'll just go for a 10 minute walk every day. And that's going to, I'll feel better. I'll improve my cognitive performance. Like I don't, there's no more there. There's nothing opened up for it. So I would, I think I would get rid of all of these. The other thing to just jump on before I hand it back to you. This is not, from my perspective, this is not an open loop to get me to read tomorrow. Did I choose a career change to embrace movement when I was busy being an independent filmmaker? More on that tomorrow. Well, obviously you did. That's what the email is talking about is, like if you didn't, this is going to be a really weird soap opera series because this, everything is about movement. And about your trip to Southern California. Like if I don't already know that you've changed careers to embrace movement, I'm not paying attention to anything. There's no open loop there. And at the end of the day, it's to sound like I'm being jerk and I don't mean this to sound like a jerk. I, as the reader, I don't really give a shit what you did. Right? I mean, yes, I do. I like the story. But what I really want to know, if you really want to open a loop that grabs my attention is what is something that about me that's valuable about me that I'm going to find out tomorrow. That's what I really want to know. So, you know, this is not the way I would use, but it's more this open loop at the end. First of all, it should be the culmination of all the tension in the email itself. Like the email should be leading to this. This is not really an open loop. It's cliffhanger. And so all of the tension in this email should lead up to this question that is a cliffhanger until tomorrow. And that question needs to be something that is of interest to the reader, not just about the writer. I think is how I would describe that. And I don't have a good idea for what that might be, but it would be something more along the lines of what did I learn about X that you can use to improve Y? Or that's not a great model, but it's something like that. It's, you know, or what did I, you know, what did I learn that I never expected? And why is that important? Why has that what and why have I realized that why that's so important? I mean, again, the wordsmithing is important here. It's the structuralist, the idea. Like funnel all the tension in this, like gradually ascend the tension in this entire, especially because it's the first one, set the stage, build the tension, focus the attention on a cliffhanger that all I want to know is what the hell happens tomorrow. Like what I, as soon as tomorrow's email comes in, I already have this question in my mind and let me jump into it. So I need anything to add to that before we move on to the next one. Yeah, I think subtext. So the whole idea of having a subtext, its power is not drawing attention to it. It's for people to feel and come to that conclusion themselves and allowing that to help them create this connection between the tension of A and B. So, you know, one of the subtexts may be this thing, oh, you know, she's clearly used to be an independent filmmaker. Now she's doing this other thing. You don't have to say much more than that other than, then, I mean, you don't have to draw attention to that explicitly. It's there in the narrative. And I think that will pull more people forward. And then the other thing, there's a few things that I liked in that last paragraph just below the bullets that's kind of hidden in that chunk of text there. I mean, it's, you know, I think that's a powerful word for people to hear, because they are probably certainly thinking it in their head to make friends with fear. That's also interesting. And then to become a happy mover, aging with confidence. That's, I don't know, that's, there's all these little pieces that I think are really nice. They just don't, they shouldn't be all crammed up inside there. Yeah, it's funny. I think, I think I would write that, I think I would write that different. Again, is it, is it lyrical, right? So, like more brave to me sticks out, because I think it should be braver, which is just, I mean, talk about really being the nuances of writing, but because you probably have no interest in walking on a rail or being a weirdo in the park. And that's cool. But the principles I learned and leveraged to be stronger, braver, less fearful. Yeah, I think this could be a, this could be smoother. Like, I don't know. I mean, or not. I mean, again, it's where it gets, I get hung up where it's, I just, I love the lyrical nature of writing. So, I learned, I learned and leveraged, the principles I learned and leveraged to be stronger, braver, less fearful. Yeah, the last thing doesn't fit. Because you can hear it, right? Like if I say, if I'm like, you know, the principles I learned and leveraged to be stronger, braver, make friends with fear, and become a happy mover aging with conga, like, what's that? But like to me, this is two things. This paragraph, and I think this is what you're feeling too. This paragraph is actually two paragraphs, two sentences. One is, you probably have no interest in walking on a rail or being a weirdo in the park. And that's cool. Period. But comma, the principles I learned and leveraged to be stronger, braver, stronger and braver, help me make friends with fear. Period. And I became a happy mover aging with confidence for my adventures in the park world. Like that to me, that sounds right to me. Like that. Is this, this is a, there's, there's beautiful language in here, but like, like, your audience isn't going to do the work to make it read that way. They're going to kind of jump through it. And I think this paragraph is too powerful. I think you need to separate it out. I don't know if that's how I would write that sentence. I can't help it at it. It's just, yeah, I think that's great. In fact, while we're on this now, just really quickly is, is if anybody's interested about really unpacking what Sean's just said about, you know, sentence being sentences being lyrical and word choice being important and, and the, the value of reading things out loud, as opposed to just reading it in your head. Get a book court steering the craft by Ursula Le Guin, I think. I think that's a name and she's, she's, she's passed on now, but it's an amazing, amazing read. It's very easy, but chapter one is all about this. So you could probably go to Kindle and just do the free chapter thing and you'll probably get the harder chapter one for free. And it just, it, it just unpacks this whole idea about writing to hear the beauty of the, of the words in a, in a lyrical pattern. And there's a few examples and worth, worth doing it if that interests you. I just psyched out of that book. So I'm really excited. Yeah, the PS here too, a couple of opportunities for the PS. This is sort of general observation stuff. Like this PS, if you all look at those early films, again, at this point in time, and we've been de-amern about it for a while, so maybe we're throwing ourselves off. I don't, I don't know how that's insert, like I don't know how looking at those early films is in service to the broader soap opera series. So I, maybe like maybe seeing people move really well is in service to that. You can also do PS, looking back at, you know, something basically like mentioning the films and saying, maybe you might share links later. Like if it's something like looking back, whatever, like looking back on those films, I don't feel like they were, you know, a little hesitant to share those because they're kind of old or whatever, but I may do that, you know, in a future email, just, just a little open loop, like, okay, like just little something to get my attention forward, but, or no PS at all. But I don't, I don't think this PS, I like, I like a PS in the first email in general. I don't know that I've ever thought about this explicitly, just because I want, I want to establish that that's likely to happen, like look to the PS, stuff's going to happen here. And I also like a PS, you can do a lot of things with a PS, you can amplify the tension, you just created a previous cliffhanger. You can have a parallel set of tension that further amplifies it in a different way. And there's so many different ways. I don't go too far, you know, we'll keep moving on. Hey, we made it through one email so far. Perfect. This, I mean, multiply whatever we've spent so far times 26, and we're good. Okay, this is Act Two. The want and the need, what are they looking for, taking somewhere new place for circumstances, they have to figure out what's happening. Hopeless to hopeful. This is such a great, this is a, this is a great example of a micro theme within an email. I love, love, love, love this. So hope, hopeless to hopeful. If you believe your audience has been burned trying something before, if something feels overwhelming from the outside, but when you break it down for them, it actually is something anybody can do. Once they understand how it works, like, oh, hopeless to hopeful is such a, such a beautiful theme. Because there are two hurdles we have to overcome to get somebody to take action, to buy whatever it is. The first hurdle we have to overcome is they have to believe that what we're saying is true and that we, we can deliver on the promise that we're making. That's the first hurdle. It's the one that's easy to cover. That's why we talk about what's in courses or like, like, that's, that's, that's the easier one. The harder one that's often unspoken is we have to overcome the prospects believe that, yes, we could do it, but they can't. There's something wrong with them. I mean, that's a very common problem among, in, in, I would say every market that a lot of people, and we see the feedback around, you know, like, how people think that the system doesn't work or the course isn't right or whatever else, but the real fear for most people isn't that you can't do it. You, the course creator, you, the product creator, it's that they can't do it. So hopeless to hopeful is a beautiful theme. It can be a theme that guides an entire soap opera series and then recurrent, you know, and one thing, not to get too weird here, but one thing to think about themes is themes can be fractal and fractal just means the, the, the thing that you're talking about repeats within itself. So a fractal theme would be the, the entire theme of this soap opera series could be hopeless to hopeful. And then it could repeat itself specifically within an email, within each email. So it doesn't have to, but it can be that way. So this is a long, long circuitous introduction to how much I love this particular theme. And I loved that it's identified because it's something that Julie can refer back to. Just kind of check yourself, you check the writing against this. Did I do the work? Did, did I establish in the beginning through dialogue, through writing, through story, through examples, hopeless? Does, does the audience know what hopeless feels like? And have I, by the end, given them reason, even if they're subtle and just the intro, have I given them a spark to be hopeful? Yes or no? Have I done the work? All right, off to you. I liked the contrast that was set up early on here with, with the, with the mention of Charlie's Angels. So there's, you know, she was her favorite thing she used to watch come back on a Tuesday evening after Brownies. Do you guys get, get Brownies over there? Is it the same name? Okay. So she comes back from Brownies and watches, watches the Charlie's Angels. These, you know, they're just normal, they're normal chicks, but, you know, they're not superheroes. But then at the same time, she writes, where is it? I progressed that it for six months before taking my first Parkour session. Yeah, I, what do I think about this one? I don't, don't talk about Parkour. That's my first thought. Way too soon. So you have to do this work that Julie has done to get all of these thoughts out of your head. And then you can start sequencing them in a way that builds and releases tension. And I think that's a good point you've drawn attention to the fact that, you know, don't beat yourself up about your, your shitty first draft. I do a draft zero. Yeah, I mean, that's the, that's the squirted dopamine. So if we have apparently every recording has to reference an oscilloscope and a squirted dopamine before it's completed. So now we're done. But that's really the, that's the reveal, right? We want the audience, we're giving them two plus two. Right? Or we're giving this, and this is, that's from the unifying, that's the unifying theory of two plus two from the Ted video that I think we have linked somewhere. So the key is to a good story or whatever. But the, when we give the, we give the audience four, they get less value out of it. And when we give them four right in the beginning, I mean, imagine the, imagine the difference in suspense. If we spend three emails establishing two, and then the next three emails establishing plus two, like the, and then at that next point, if we just give them four, we've relieved the tension. But if we, if we give them two plus two, and then we give them, you know, another three emails where we're giving them clues that the two and the two equals something, and then they realize, oh, shit, it's parkour, right? Like they, like they figured out, they get that, that emotional aha moment. Now contrast this with what happens in this soap opera series, which is we give them four right in the beginning. We're like, Hey, it's four. And then we start giving them two, and then we tell them again, as we're giving them two, whoops, that it's four. And then we give them a couple of more things to establish the next two, but we keep reminding them that it's four, that by the time we get to, you know, email six or whatever, there's, there's nothing to reveal. There's nothing for them to imagine. There's no, we've already told them the whole thing, we've solved the equation for them. There's nothing for them to do. So if from a conceptual standpoint, this series really wants to be three emails establishing the first part, which is, you know, you can imagine that as the initial two, like what are the, what's the background? So like that's, that's the first two. I think it's really digging deep into like, what, how did she get like, what was that, how did she get there? She first started, her first memory of that is Charlie's Angels. They were like superheroes with no superpowers. So there was something there. And so the next few emails are the net, it's the second part of two plus two. The first part is Julie, where she is, how she feels. Second part is the vision of what she knows is possible that she's seen over the course of her life. Then don't rush and give me four and tell me it's parkour that got you there. Begin to take me on the journey that led you to parkour. You can even describe what you did. Just don't ever use the word. Describe, you know, this parkour is, people who don't know parkour are also called free running parkour is a French term. But it's also, I believe it translates into free running. I don't know if it's a direct translation, but you've, if you've seen parkour, it's this very dynamic outside, very natural type of movement. So Julie can describe the actual movement. She can describe reading the book Born to Run and running barefoot and be interested in that. I read Born to Run. I had a very similar experience, but you can, she can describe all of those things, but never say parkour because if she doesn't, that's the four that the audience has to fill in for themselves. Because if they can imagine, if they can actually see Julie running barefoot out in a park, like at some point, someone who is familiar with it is going to go, that's, it sounds like parkour, right? That's when they're like, aha, I figured out what she's talking about. Right? Like that, and that feels good as the reader. And then at some point, she can bring it all together. She can name it in passing and mention that, you know, this has a name and this is what it's about. And this is why it's so valuable. And this is what it does for people who do it because, but now, when she, when she reveals it way later, she has captured my attention so thoroughly. And that's the business we're in. We're in the business of sustained attention. Right? And the way to a sustained attention is to have the thing that's going to eventually resolve the tension far enough in the future that I'm buying it for the whole journey. That's what this, that's what I feel like is really missing here is all of the parts are here. All of the writing is here. It's just not in the right order. And it's revealed too soon. I think that's my, that's my gut assessment of this one. Yeah, definitely agree. By the time people, by the time you mentioned parkour, they should be, yeah, I knew that already. Yeah, there's, there's just so much here. I feel like at this point, I'm just, I don't know, I think we're just going to start repeating ourselves. But I think what you said earlier, what you just reiterated there is so much of the magic that's easy to forget, which is, let people have a sense of discovery. That's so important. I forget that it's certainly if I have an Achilles' Yield, it's that like, it's, it's, I want to get to the thing and talk about the thing because the thing is so interesting to me. And I think not talking about the thing, but talking about the, how you got there, why the thing is, you know, like, imply, and this is what, this is where I struggle so much, because I'm so precise in my writing, that it's, it feels so uncomfortable to me to be talking about something and not explicitly name it, because it's just, I don't think that way. But that's, it's such an important insight for our audience to hear is that that's really the job that we're doing is we're, we're setting things up so that eventually the person has that sense of discovery. It's such a good word that they feel like what, when you say it, there's almost a sense of like, I knew it. I knew that's what you were talking about. Because that feels good. You feel like a hero. I mean, if you think, and it's such, I mean, it's what a great, what a way to make your audience feel excellent is to just, it is to make them feel smart, to make them feel like a, to engineer a sequence of emails, a series of emails, where the payoff is for them to feel smart before you reveal something to them. That's, I mean, that's brilliant on so many levels. I just, I'm fixated on something here. So I want to, want to focus on this. This is my own mania. So take this with a grain of salt. But here's this paragraph, right? So I also had a serendipitous encounter with my old Tai Chi teacher on Ladbrook Grove, introduced me to Rolfing. Rolfing, Rolfing. Yeah, I did Rolfing. So I should not have pronounced it. Fashion manipulation. As he fixed three years of bilateral Achilles tendonitis, 30 minutes, sweaty, palmed, Chinese burns type session that made me swear to him a lot, like good friends do. This, this is a really interesting paragraph. I have no idea. I have no idea if it adds any value whatsoever. And I'm, I feel like I've had to work so hard to get through that that I just be cautious of these kinds of paragraphs. It's one of those things like it's really easy to add something that feels like we're kind of adding some color to, and if you're going to, if you're going to add this, you need to make it a lot more succinct. You know, it's, and because this is a lot, and maybe this is just how you write naturally. And this is your first draft, but there's a lot here. You know, it could be, this could be a sentence. You know, I also had a serendipitous encounter with my old high T, my old Tai Chi teacher who've, you know, fixed three years of ankle problems in 30 minutes, and it hurt like hell, right? Like, make it easy to understand. Because I don't know what half of this means. I don't know what Achilles tendonitis is, kind of an idea. If you don't know what Rolfine is, then that's a challenge too. If you don't, and if, and if Burs, if you don't know what fascia is, now you're like doubly confused. Like, there's just so much here to derail things. Like say things in a way that you, that you could just say to somebody very succinctly in a conversation. Like, if Julie and I ran into each other right after this happened, the way she wouldn't say it to me this way. She was like, oh, you're never going to believe this. I just ran into my old Tai Chi teacher. And, and, and, and, you know, this, this is how this conversation would, would happen between two friends and treat your audience like friends. So if I ran into Julie on the street, she would, first of all, she would not say serendipitous, right? People don't talk like that. They, you might say what something that means serendipitous, but you probably wouldn't use the word. But she would probably say like, you're never going to believe this. I just ran into my old Tai Chi teacher. My ankle's been killing me for three years, and he did this weird thing where he like, you know, this weird, like, you know, Chinese mysticism did this thing like 30 minutes later, it feels fine. Like that's so weird, right? Like that's, that's how two people would talk to each other about something like this. So just go back to the, and I screw this up all the time too. So this is me talking to myself. Just remember when you're, when you're sharing something with somebody, think about how you would tell a friend and at least get that out of your head onto the page and then kind of mess with it from there. All right. I have this feeling that anything we say beyond much longer, we're going to start get making things worse. So yeah, so let me just say, you know, let's wrap things up. You say so. I think I'll say like one tiny thing at the end. But you take us home here because I think I'm tired of your mailing voice. So that, that sentence that we've just, that you've just unpacked that with the rolfing. When I'm writing my, my drafts eros and the stories that seem interesting show up on the page. I don't judge them in the moment. I just get them out. And many times they'll come out like this, where there's just, there's just too much details that are irrelevant. And, you know, I've always found it very difficult to, to kill, kill my babies. What's the saying, but something like that. Kill your darlings. Darling, that's it. And, you know, one of the exercises in the book that I, that I cited early on, because of then every chapter, there's, there's a few writing exercises. If you want to do the exercises and one of the, one of the ones she, she mentions is you should be able to read almost any book, take any story and the exercises make it half as long. So cut it in, cut it in half and retain all the meaning. And it's a, you know, it's a difficult thing to do, but it can certainly be done. And in many cases, the story is better for it. So yeah. So just below that. That's my job is to kill your darlings. And it's so funny. And we never talk about this, but I always feel bad. I mean, I feel bad right up until I don't, but, you know, from our writing process, you couldn't have two different writers from a process perspective. But always giving show on darlings. Yeah. I mean, yeah, you give, yeah, you give me like, you know, often when Andre does the first book, but I consider the base writing, but one of us usually writes the base, which is just the thing. And then we collaborate on it. But I, there are just so many times and I like, I get handed something and I just, and I feel, I feel bad for a moment. But it's like, yeah, half of this just disappears in the first, the first five minutes, I just, it's gone. And it's, and it's, and it's not because, it's not, it's not because the writing's bad. It's not, it's just, it just becomes the, are the, like, what's the main thing that we're talking about? And what is necessary? What's, what is required in service to that main point? And what is it? And, and then you just take it away. And it's, I don't remember the quotes from it. You know, I think it was from the author of the Little Prince, who's one of my favorite quotes. And I can't pronounce his name because his name is French. So I never do. But that it's this idea that beauty is not when there's nothing left to add. It's when there's nothing left to take away like that. And it's such a different distinction because we think, I mean, if you think about this, Michelangelo's David, the statue, like David is, that there was a block of stone and inside, and this apparently this quote is not accurate, but there was this idea that Michelangelo's just removed everything that wasn't David and what remained was the statue, right? But that's kind of what, that's beauty, right? The things that are not David have been removed. So a lot of editing is not, you're just, you're taking away the things that are not required for the beauty that's there to reveal itself in a very powerful way. Now, you can choose to add more. It's, and oftentimes there's a great reason to do that and that you can elaborate on things in that, but to have that, there's a real elegant beauty, certainly influenced by my background as a journalist, put that in perspective, but there's a real beauty to be able to remove 50, 60, 70% of a piece of writing and just see what remains. Now, it doesn't mean that's the final, but it gives you a structure, which oftentimes the final can be built. So there's, Julie doesn't have to remove this paragraph, but it's an opportunity to ask yourself two questions. One, does this belong in general? Does this further the narrative that I've created for my audience in a meaningful way? And if the answer to that is no, remove it. If the answer to that is yes, the next question is, how can I make this better? And this is one, this can be made better by making a one sentence. Removing a lot of all the jargon and the details. This can just be one sentence that says the same thing. So that was a fun tangent. Thanks. Thanks for setting that one up. Sorry, I killed so many of your darlings. Well, that's why I sent them to you because I'll call them. It's all right. Just to be fair though, I usually move your darlings. I don't kill them all just so that the audience doesn't feel like, why are you working with that jackass? Like, to be clear for everybody is listening. I tend to structure Andres darlings. Sometimes I put them in different places, but very rarely do any get killed. We don't kill their darlings around here. All right. Say something smart to wrap this up so we can hand this off to Julie. Yeah. And then on that bombshell, I mean, she's got so many nice phrases and words here that I can't wait to read this once it's been redone again. This idea, like this visual idea of not running in straight lines. We should put you macro bravery. You can think about stuff like that. It feels attainable, this macro bravery, that idea and not running in straight lines. It's just there's so much here that I think can be strong when it's wrapped in a narrative that's a lot more cleaner when you get there. So, I mean, we're only on page, well, page two or three now of many. So, Julie's got lots of work to do. And I think this has been broadly helpful for everybody, even though, you know, there's a different story. I think most of the things we've spoken about, we all face every single time we stare at a blank page and start to spew out something, especially when we've done our draft zero or draft one. And kind of, you know, it's taking shape, but it's still far from what needs to happen before it can be stuck in front of somebody. So, yeah, I think that's what I'll end on. I think this is really good. It's beautiful. Yeah, this is just, I mean, it's, this is so much work that went into this. And just thinking about, I'm just, and I don't know how Julie did this, but I'm imagining that she put her butt in a chair and wrote 26 pages. And that is an act of macro bravery. That is an act of sustained bravery in conviction. And that, that's the work. I mean, we can say this a million times in a million different ways, but it all comes down to this, this simple thing you have to do the work. And there's no way around that. So, there are different ways to do the work. I wouldn't have written this series the way that Julie did. But the way I would have written it, is it better, it isn't worse. It's just unique to how I write. The fact that she sat down and did this is profound, absolutely profound, because now she's in the easy stage. She's in the easy stage. It's just sequencing thoughts and putting them in a way that's coherent. It's part of the work. But you can't sequence something that doesn't exist. You can't structure something that doesn't exist. So, what she's done here is, I mean, I couldn't be more impressed. There's, at the macro level, 26 pages of content to arrange into a soap opera series. At the micro level, there are these beautiful, like vignettes throughout the entirety of this that can be, they're so, and they could, they could inspire new series, they could be subtext within the larger, there's so much to work with here. And at the nano level, there are these beautiful sentences and beautiful expressions of thought that are, there's just so much here. So, this was, this was a real privilege. Everything is, anytime reviewing anybody's work, is a privilege. But this one is just so much fun to see somebody take the work so seriously and to produce something of this magnitude is just profound. So, I'll leave, I'll leave that when they thank you to Julie. And thank you for giving us this opportunity to read this work because this was, this was a real privilege. All right, let's, let's wrap it up for the day. Thank you, everybody. Thanks, everyone. We can go get these dresses off now.
